{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.3,
    "R1.2": 0.7,
    "R1.3": 1.0,
    "R1.4": 0.3,
    "R1.5": 0.3,
    "R1.6": 0.7,
    "R1.7": 0.7,
    "RX.1": 1.0,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 1.0,
    "RX.5": 1.0
  },
  "active_set": ["R1.2", "R1.3", "R1.6", "R1.7", "RX.1", "RX.2", "RX.3", "RX.4", "RX.5"],
  "rationale": {
    "R1.2": "The conclusion asserts RegRank 'delivers positive net returns,' but the reported figures show RegRank yields $124 − $187 = −$63/year net versus the baseline's $89 − $96 = −$7/year net — RegRank is strictly worse on the paper's own numbers. This premise-to-conclusion break is a central defect that 'improving' the draft must confront.",
    "R1.3": "The quantitative evidence in the results paragraph directly contradicts the affirmative conclusion; reconciling or correcting this misalignment is load-bearing for the draft's validity and is therefore the highest-priority improvement task.",
    "R1.6": "'Improve this draft' canonically activates prose-level work; sentence clarity, cohesion, and register improvements are expected across all paragraphs regardless of technical scope.",
    "R1.7": "The degradation model explicitly cites Peterson et al. (2010) for NMC cycling coefficients and adopts them for an LFP fleet. LFP and NMC exhibit materially different degradation profiles; this is both a citation-accuracy and methodological-attribution issue that bears on the soundness of the quantitative results."
  },
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "Should the improvement address substantive technical issues (internal numerical contradictions, chemistry/citation mismatches) in addition to surface prose quality, or is the scope limited to writing polish only?"
}

rationale.R1.2: The conclusion asserts RegRank 'delivers positive net returns,' but the reported figures show RegRank yields $124 − $187 = −$63/year net versus the baseline's $89 − $96 = −$7/year net — RegRank is strictly worse on the paper's own numbers. This premise-to-conclusion break is a central defect that 'improving' the draft must confront.
rationale.R1.3: The quantitative evidence in the results paragraph directly contradicts the affirmative conclusion; reconciling or correcting this misalignment is load-bearing for the draft's validity and is therefore the highest-priority improvement task.
rationale.R1.6: 'Improve this draft' canonically activates prose-level work; sentence clarity, cohesion, and register improvements are expected across all paragraphs regardless of technical scope.
rationale.R1.7: The degradation model explicitly cites Peterson et al. (2010) for NMC cycling coefficients and adopts them for an LFP fleet. LFP and NMC exhibit materially different degradation profiles; this is both a citation-accuracy and methodological-attribution issue that bears on the soundness of the quantitative results.

parsed.clarification_question: Should the improvement address substantive technical issues (internal numerical contradictions, chemistry/citation mismatches) in addition to surface prose quality, or is the scope limited to writing polish only?
